response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a carpenter in Philadelphia, I spend about an hour a day on social media, mostly to unwind after work or connect with friends, and I've seen my share of fake content. It's frustrating, like when you're building something and someone hands you the wrong tool—it just slows everything down. My approach is pretty hands-on, just like my job. I don't have formal education, but I've learned to trust my gut and double-check things. When I see a post that seems off, maybe about a local event or a health tip, I'll take a minute to look it up on a few different sites or ask someone I know who's more tech-savvy. I'm curious by nature, so I enjoy digging into the details, but my low openness score means I'm not quick to believe everything new without proof. Being self-employed, I value honesty and reliability, so I try to share only what I'm sure about, to avoid spreading misinformation. Social media platforms could do better by making it easier to report fake stuff and showing clearer sources, but ultimately, it's on us users to be careful. I think staying skeptical and talking things through with others, like when I volunteer or play football, helps keep things real. It's not perfect, but it works for me without taking up too much time.",

Hobbies like volunteering and football reinforce community interaction. Overall, opinion emphasizes personal vigilance, social checks, and practical solutions over technical fixes.",
  "tone": "Practical, conversational, slightly skeptical, community-oriented, and grounded in personal experience.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a carpenter in Philadelphia, I spend about an hour a day on social media, mostly to unwind after work or connect with friends, and I've seen my share of fake content. It's frustrating, like when you're building something and someone hands you the wrong tool—it just slows everything down. My approach is pretty hands-on, just like my job. I don't have formal education, but I've learned to trust my gut and double-check things. When I see a post that seems off, maybe about a local event or a health tip, I'll take a minute to look it up on a few different sites or ask someone I know who's more tech-savvy. I'm curious by nature, so I enjoy digging into the details, but my low openness score means I'm not quick to believe everything new without proof. Being self-employed, I value honesty and reliability, so I try to share only what I'm sure about, to avoid spreading misinformation. Social media platforms could do better by making it easier to report fake stuff and showing clearer sources, but ultimately, it's on us users to be careful. I think staying skeptical and talking things through with others, like when I volunteer or play football, helps keep things real. It's not perfect, but it works for me without taking up too much time.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: Age 36 and occupation as carpenter suggest practical, hands-on approach. No formal education implies reliance on experience and informal learning. High extraversion (score 98) indicates social engagement, leading to emphasis on discussing with others. Low openness (score 27) suggests skepticism towards new information, aligning with cautious verification. High neuroticism (score 77) may contribute to frustration with fake content. Moderate conscientiousness (score 66) supports responsible sharing. Time on social media (one hour daily) frames limited but focused usage. Hobbies like volunteering and football reinforce community interaction. Overall, opinion emphasizes personal vigilance, social checks, and practical solutions over technical fixes.
response_parsed.tone: Practical, conversational, slightly skeptical, community-oriented, and grounded in personal experience.
